Verse 1.136.translation: Non-intention of what is present, subservience to another, indication of a meaning through a mark — such manifold interpretive principle is articulated by reasoning.
Verse 1.136.commentary: What the licensed tarka of 1.135 actually does, shown by its instruments. The verse names three canons from the interpreter's working kit: sato 'vivaksa — a real feature of the situation may be *unintended*, present but not meant (the speaker mentions the man's staff without meaning staffness); pararthya — an element may stand wholly in service of another, its sense exhausted in subordination (the Mimamsa principle governing subsidiary and principal); vyaktir laingiki — a meaning may be delivered only through its linga, its indicatory mark, never said outright. Each canon adjudicates precisely the gap 1.135 identified between form and meaning: form alone cannot tell you what was not-intended, what is subordinate, what is marked-at — judgement must. Iti nyayo bahuvidhah: and such maxims are many; tarkena pravibhajyate, reasoning is what articulates and applies them. The verse is a window onto the actual exegetical culture — Mimamsa's interpretive jurisprudence — that the kanda's abstractions have presupposed throughout: 'tradition needs an eye' is no metaphor; here are the lens-grindings.
Verse 1.136.contested: the three items are compressed to the point of riddle and translators divide sharply; we take them as three named canons of interpretation (unintendedness of the actual, subservience, mark-borne indication), but each compound admits alternative resolution — e.g. sato 'vivaksa as 'non-assertion of the existent' in the ontological sense — and the verse may be quoting a list whose original context is lost

Verse 1.137.translation: That power belongs to words themselves; the reasoning that rests on a person, when it follows the words, is interpretive principle — apart from the traditions it is without ground.
Verse 1.137.commentary: The settlement of reason's status in one careful sentence. The interpretive maxims of 1.136 work — but their sakti, their authority, sabdanam eva: belongs to the words themselves, to the structure of the received language they explicate. Tarka as it lives in a reasoner — purusasraya, person-based, the wit of Devadatta — is authoritative only sabda-anugata, when it follows the words: tracking, subserving, drawing out. Cut loose — anagamesu, where there is no received text to follow — it is anibandhana, groundless: the same activity that was an eye within tradition (1.135) is a wanderer without it. This resolves the apparent zigzag of the kanda's epistemology (reason demolished 1.32-42, licensed 1.135-136): there were never two doctrines, only one — reasoning is real as an *instrument of the word* and nothing as an independent source. The formulation is also quietly self-referential: the Vakyapadiya itself, bristling with arguments, claims authority not as one man's tarka but as reasoning anugata to the grammarians' agama — the book practices the epistemology it preaches.
Verse 1.137.contested: we read the verse as *subordinating* personal reasoning to word-grounded principle; it can also be read as *identifying* them ('the nyaya just is that word-power as refracted through persons'), a more charitable unity — the practical upshot (no freestanding authority for tarka) is the same

Verse 1.138.translation: As colour and the rest are seen to have their powers fixed with respect to each object, just so are words seen to have theirs — in the removal of poison and the like.
Verse 1.138.commentary: Evidence that words have real, object-fixed powers — from the mantra. The analogy's base: sensible qualities have yata-sakti, determinate powers indexed to their bearers (this herb's colour signals this virtue; fire's touch burns and water's cools, invariably). The claim: words exhibit the same fixity, and the evidence offered is visapaharana-adi — the removal of poison and such: the practice of mantra-healing, where *this* formula and no paraphrase of it counteracts *this* venom. Whatever a modern reader makes of the practice, its logic is the argument's point: mantra-efficacy is form-bound — substitute a synonym and the power is gone — which is inexplicable if words are arbitrary labels whose whole being is conventional reference, but exactly what one expects if particular word-forms carry particular saktis as natively as colours carry theirs. The verse recruits the tradition's applied linguistics (the whole culture of mantra-sastra) as empirical support for the ontology of 1.13 and 1.128: words are powers before they are signs.
Verse 1.138.contested: the wisdomlib transcription's yatasaktayah we read as yata-sakti ('fixed powers', as at 1.6); the sceptical reader will note the argument is only as strong as the mantra-datum, which Bhartrhari's audience granted and ours may not — the karika's own force is conditional on that concession

Verse 1.139.translation: As their capacity is acknowledged there, so let it be acknowledged with respect to dharma as well; therefore what is correct is to be spoken by the correct-speaking who seek felicity.
Verse 1.139.commentary: The inference from 1.138 drawn, and the kanda's practical ethics stated. If words have real form-bound powers in the visible domain (poison-cure), yatha esam tatra samarthyam, then evam dharme 'pi pratiyatam — let the same be acknowledged where the fruit is unseen: in dharma. The step is the standard extension from drsta to adrsta efficacy that ritual reasoning always makes (1.7's dual purposes), here applied to speech-forms themselves. Conclusion, marked tasmat: sadhunam sadhubhir vacyam abhyudaya-arthinam — correct words are to be spoken, by the cultivated, for those (or: by those) who seek abhyudaya, felicity, the rising good. The verse completes the arc opened at 1.27: there, correct and corrupt forms conveyed meaning equally and correctness was dharma, on authority; now the doctrine has its mechanism (words as powers, 1.138) and its imperative (speak the sadhu form — the power is in the form, so the form is the practice). Grammar's rules are hereby cashed as ethics: usage is conduct, and conjugation, rightly done, is a small continuous sacrifice.
Verse 1.139.contested: sadhubhih can be instrumental of agent ('by the cultivated') or of accompaniment/means, and abhyudaya-arthinam genitive plural can attach to the speakers or beneficiaries; the injunction's sense — right forms for the sake of felicity — is stable across the parsings

Verse 1.140.translation: Everyone arrives at objects whose fruit is unseen through tradition; and about tradition, the contrary can everywhere be alleged.
Verse 1.140.commentary: A concession of disarming honesty, placed exactly where the argument is strongest. First line: adrsta-phala objects — dharma, the felicities of 1.139, everything whose fruit no one living has seen — are reached agamat, through tradition, by *everyone*: there is no other road (1.30 again, now as sociology). Second line: viparitam ca sarvatra sakyate vaktum agame — and about any tradition, the opposite can always be said. Counter-traditions exist; every agama has a rival alleging its inversion; no received teaching is immune to contradiction by another received teaching. Bhartrhari does not blink the consequence: tradition-dependence cannot be *demonstrated* into safety; the sceptic can always speak. What follows for him is not suspension but the practical resolution already given — one stands in the unbroken usage of one's sistas (1.31, 1.141 next) because standing somewhere is not optional and brokenness is the only disqualifier available. The verse is the kanda's moment of epistemic candour: the ground of practice is firm enough to live on and too contested to prove, and the philosophy of tradition must be written for exactly that terrain.
Verse 1.140.contested: the transcription sarvo 'drphalanarthan is corrupt at drpha-; context and the parallel constructions make adrsta-phalan arthan ('objects of unseen fruit') the evident reading, and we translate so while noting the source's fault

Verse 1.141.translation: This grammatical tradition is that whose object is the knowledge of correctness; it is grounded in the unbroken remembrance of the cultivated.
Verse 1.141.commentary: The kanda's definition of its own discipline, quiet after the grand claims. Vyakarana-smrti — grammar as a *smrti*, a remembrance-tradition, not a sruti: the classification is itself doctrine (1.7 made smrtis derivative of the Veda via lingas; grammar is such a derivative, the sistas' codified memory). Its visaya: sadhutva-jnana, knowledge of which forms are correct — not meaning (which corrupt forms convey equally, 1.27), not communication, but correctness as such, the dharma-bearing property. Its ground: avicchedena sistanam — the *unbrokenness* of the cultivated line, idam smrti-nibandhanam, this is what fastens the remembrance. Every term has been earned: aviccheda from 1.31 and 1.41 (the immunity of the unbroken), sista from 1.27 (usage of the learned as criterion), nibandhana as the kanda's own word for grounding since 1.13. The verse is the discipline's charter written in its own vocabulary — and its modesty matters: after word-Brahman and cosmic seeds, grammar's proximate authority is simply that the chain of cultivated speakers never broke.

Verse 1.142.translation: This is the wonder: that beyond the threefold speech — vaikhari, madhyama and pasyanti, with their many distinct fords — there is the supreme station of the Word.
Verse 1.142.commentary: The kanda's celebrated hierarchy, named at last. The stages have been operating incognito since 1.112-117 (utterance; the word in mind and breath; the knower in the subtle speech-self); now the names: vaikhari, the elaborated — audible, articulated, sequence-ridden speech; madhyama, the middle — the inner word, mentally deployed, sequenced in thought though unsounded; pasyanti, the seeing — speech at the station where it *sees* rather than says: undivided, sequence-swallowed, intentionality without articulation. Aneka-tirtha-bheda: each level has many fords, many descents and crossings — dialects and deliveries at the gross end, modes of inner discourse and vision at the subtle. And the adbhuta, the declared wonder: trayyah vacah param padam — beyond even the triad, a supreme station of speech. The wording is a deliberate re-sounding of the oldest text in this lineage: the Rgveda's four quarters of vac, three hidden and the fourth what men speak (RV 1.164.45), and the padam of catvari padani. Whether that fourth is para vac as a distinct level (as the later tradition, especially Kashmir Saiva, systematized it — para as a fourth above pasyanti) or pasyanti itself in its absolute aspect is left genuinely open by the karika. The doctrinal work is done either way: speech is depth-structured, its audible face is its furthest emanation, and the sabda-tattva of 1.1 stands at (or as) the summit of an accessible gradient — which makes the return-path of 1.131 (union by the word's perfecting) a climb through one's own speaking, from voice through mind to vision.
Verse 1.142.contested: whether the karika teaches three levels with a 'supreme station' identical to pasyanti's summit, or implies the four-level scheme (with para vac) the commentators and the Saiva tradition made canonical, is the classical crux of the verse; the Vrtti already reads toward the higher systematization, but the karika's own syntax — 'the param padam of the threefold vac' — supports the minimal reading, and we translate minimally

Verse 1.143.translation: That, whether undergoing division or non-division, remains established; and the powers of words are seen by those who know the nature of things.
Verse 1.143.commentary: The invariance of the summit through its own descents. Tat — the supreme word-station of 1.142 — kriyamanam vibhaga-avibhagabhyam, being done into division and non-division, worked through both conditions (uttered into vaikhari's differentiation, gathered into pasyanti's unity), avasthitam: stands, unaltered. The doctrine is 1.75-77 and 1.101 lifted to the top of the hierarchy — as the sphota is undivided through fast and slow delivery, the param padam is unmoved through the entire cycle of speech's emanation and return; levels are its vrtti-bheda, never its own change. Second line: sabda-saktayah, the powers of words — the whole system of them, from mantra-efficacy (1.138) to world-binding (1.118) — drsyante svabhava-jnaih bhavanam: are *seen* by knowers of the natures of things. Drsyante is the verb of 1.37-38: this is vision-knowledge, the rsi's mode — the deep powers of speech are not inferred from usage but beheld by those whose cognition has climbed the very gradient 1.142 mapped. The verse thus locks hierarchy to epistemology: each level of speech is also a station of knowing, and the powers operative at a level are visible only from at or above it.
Verse 1.143.contested: the referent of tat and the syntax of kriyamanam avasthitam are elliptical; we take 'that' as the param padam persisting through division/non-division, but the pair has also been construed as smrti-work ('being made, through analysis and synthesis, it stands established'), continuing 1.141's charter — the transcription's ambiguity leaves both live

Verse 1.144.translation: They declare sruti to be beginningless, unbroken, without author; smrti, however, being composed by the cultivated, is yet not discontinued.
Verse 1.144.commentary: The two canons given their formal statuses, completing 1.132's threading of the needle. Sruti: anadi, avyavacchinna, akartrka — beginningless, uninterrupted, authorless: the seed-order of 1.132 in its manifestation, received by seers (1.37), never made. Smrti: sistair nibadhyamana — *presently being composed*, the participle notably continuous: the cultivated are always writing it, codifying usage and duty age by age (grammar itself among them, 1.141) — tu na vyavacchidyate, and yet it too is never severed: authored at every point, unbroken as a stream. The two eternities are thus of different types — sruti eternal in substance (or as the recurrent seed), smrti eternal only as continuity of composition, the praninam-analogy of 1.28 institutionalized. The pair resolves the tension 1.132 staged (nothing authorless received; something survives all authors): sruti is the surviving seed, smrti the perpetual re-authoring around it, and the authority of the second derives from unbroken contact with the first (1.7's lingas). The grammarian's own book — this book — is smrti in exactly this sense: made, dated, mortal, and a link in a chain that is none of those things.

Verse 1.145.translation: Of the things unrolled out of the undivided, the naming in sruti is as in a dream; smrti, however, is framed from the indicatory marks, after knowing the reality of things.
Verse 1.145.commentary: A daring epistemology of revelation, run through the kanda's own cosmology. The bhavas, things, are vivrtta avibhagat — unrolled out of the undivided (the vivarta of 1.1, the descent of 1.112ff.). Their abhikhya, their naming-and-appearing in sruti, is svapnavat: *as in a dream*. The comparison is precise, not deflationary: dream-presentation is immediate, involuntary, whole — the dreamer does not compose the dream but undergoes its self-showing; so the rsi does not construct the Veda but receives the undivided's own self-unrolling into name (1.37's vision, now given its phenomenology — and note that 1.1's Vrtti had already reached for the dream-analogy for the world-appearance itself). Smrti by contrast is work: bhava-tattvam vijnaya, having *understood* the reality, lingebhyah vihita — framed from marks, inferentially, deliberately (1.7 verbatim). Revelation is thus dream-like showing; tradition-literature is waking construction upon it — two cognitive modes, not two grades of one. The pairing dignifies both: sruti's authority is its unconstructedness, smrti's is its method.
Verse 1.145.contested: svapnavat is the crux: we read it as characterizing the *mode* of sruti's reception (immediate self-presentation, like dream), not its truth-value; a reader who presses the simile toward illusion makes the verse subversive of sruti in a way the whole kanda forbids — yet the choice of so double-edged a comparison is itself surely deliberate

Verse 1.146.translation: The impurities that stand settled in body, speech and mind — their purifications are through the sciences of medicine, grammar and the self.
Verse 1.146.commentary: The triad of disciplines, mapped to the triad of faults. Malas, impurities, station themselves in kaya, vac, buddhi — body, speech, intellect: the tradition's standard trio of act-channels (karma is done by body, word and mind). Each has its cathartic science: cikitsa, medicine, for the body's disorders; laksana, 'the science of characteristics' — grammar's own honorific title, the discipline of laksya and laksana, usage and rule — for speech; adhyatma-sastra, the science of the self, for the mind's. The verse institutionalizes 1.14's metaphor (grammar as cikitsita of speech-faults): grammar is not *like* medicine; it occupies the middle seat in a single therapeutic system of the person, flanked by medicine and metaphysics. And the ordering carries the kanda's conviction: speech stands between body and spirit as the hinge-channel — the vag-malas (the apabhramsas about to be discussed, 1.147ff.) are neither physical disease nor mental error but a third species of impurity, with its own pathology, its own materia medica (the sista usage), and its own physicians. Purity of speech is thereby made as real, and as consequential, as health and wisdom.

Verse 1.147.translation: The word devoid of the refinement — when 'gauh' is what one intends to employ — that they call apabhramsa, though it settles on the distinguished meaning.
Verse 1.147.commentary: The formal definition of the corrupt form, with its crucial concession renewed. A speaker intends the correct gauh, 'cow'; what leaves him is gavi or goni — samskara-hina, lacking the refinement grammar names (1.131's sabda-samskara, here in its technical sense). That deviation is apabhramsa, the 'fallen-away' form. And the definition's honest clause: visistartha-nivesinam — the fallen form *settles on the distinguished meaning perfectly well*; the hearer gets the cow. Corruption is thus defined against intention and norm, not against communicative success — exactly 1.27's doctrine, now with the mechanism the intervening kanda supplied: the apabhramsa conveys, but conveys *through* the sadhu form it deviates from (as 1.152 will say), like a mispronounced name that reaches its bearer only because the right name is recoverable through it. The dossier 1.147-155 that this opens — the theory of linguistic corruption — is the kanda's most linguistically observant stretch: language change, register, and the sociolinguistics of prestige are all about to appear, inside a normative frame.
Verse 1.147.contested: prayuyuksite ('when one intends to employ') marks the definition as intention-relative: the same phonetic form is apabhramsa only against a meant sadhu target — which raises, without answering, the status of forms native to speakers who intend nothing else; 1.153-154 return to exactly this edge

Verse 1.148.translation: Words like asva and goni are correct in another domain; correctness everywhere is settled by difference of ground.
Verse 1.148.commentary: The relativity of correctness, stated with complete technical calm. Goni as 'cow' is apabhramsa; goni as 'sack' is sadhu — same phonetic form, different visaya, different verdict. Asva likewise (the traditional pair: as 'non-owner', a perfectly formed negative compound, versus dialectal deformation of asva, 'horse'). Sarvatra, everywhere, sadhutvam nimitta-bhedat vyavasthitam: correctness is fixed by the *ground* of use — the meaning-domain, the derivational warrant — not by the sound in itself. No form is corrupt as acoustics; corruption is a relation between form, meaning and derivational title. The verse quietly explodes any essentialist reading of the mala-doctrine (1.146): the impurity is not *in* the syllables, as a stain is in cloth — it is a failure of entitlement, a form appearing where it holds no warrant, like a true coin passed in the wrong currency. This sharpens rather than softens the norm: since correctness is relational, only the smrti's map of grounds (1.141) can adjudicate it, and the ear alone never suffices.
Verse 1.148.contested: the source's asvagonyadayah we resolve as asva-goni-adayah with the traditional examples; the exact pair matters little — the doctrine of nimitta-bheda is the verse's whole cargo

Verse 1.149.translation: Among correct words, those are causes of the arising of cognition by way of inference; they illuminate the word's meaning as if having attained identity with it.
Verse 1.149.commentary: How the corrupt form manages to mean — the semantics of deviation, first half. Te, the apabhramsas (continuing 1.147-148): amid the sadhus they cause understanding anumanena, by way of inference — the hearer, receiving gavi, recovers gauh as what this speaker, in this mouth-habit, must intend, and the meaning arrives through that recovery; the corrupt form is evidence of the correct one, not its equal. Yet phenomenologically — and the verse is careful to honour this — the deviant form illuminates tadatmyam upagamya iva, *as if* it had become identical with the meaning: the hearer of a familiar solecism feels no inference happening, the sense simply appears. The iva does its usual precise work: seeming-identity, actual mediation. The account is a fine piece of pragmatics — meaning through reconstruction-so-practiced-it-vanishes — and it preserves both halves of 1.27's data: communicative equality in *feel*, normative asymmetry in *mechanism*. The sadhu signifies directly; the apabhramsa signifies through the sadhu, and 1.150 will name the consequence.
Verse 1.149.contested: we take te as the apabhramsas working 'among the sadhus' (sadhusu locative of context); the verse is also construed with sadhus as subject — correct words as inferential causes — which inverts the doctrine and fits 1.150 poorly; the passage's flow decides for the first

Verse 1.150.translation: The correct forms are not followed by the cultivated as if synonyms of the corrupt; since that is so by the science of tradition, the corrupt are therefore not directly expressive.
Verse 1.150.commentary: The consequence drawn, against the leveller. If corrupt and correct forms both convey the meaning (1.147, 1.149), why not call them paryayas, synonyms — parallel names of one thing, as hasta and kara both name the hand? Because, the verse answers, the sistas do not so treat them: na anugamyante paryaya iva — in cultivated usage the pair never behaves as a synonym-pair does (either member freely chosen, neither corrective of the other); gavi is *corrected to* gauh, never the reverse, and the asymmetry is codified smrti-sastrena, by the received science (1.141). Therefore: saksat avacakah — the corrupt forms are not *directly* expressive; their conveying is the mediated, inferential kind of 1.149. The argument-form deserves notice: a semantic thesis (direct versus mediated expression) is established from a *sociological* datum (the direction of correction among the cultivated) certified by smrti — exactly the epistemology 1.27-31 and 1.137 licensed, where the practice of the sistas is the deep evidence and theory follows it. The synonym-test itself is genuinely sharp linguistics: substitutability-without-correction remains a working criterion for synonymy.
Verse 1.150.contested: the ablative/instrumental construction yatah smrti-sastrena is compressed; we read 'since (their non-parity) is so by the smrti', others read 'since they are not followed (as expressive) *because of* the smrti' — making the smrti the cause rather than the certifier of the asymmetry; the difference matters for how conventionalist one takes the doctrine to be

Verse 1.151.translation: As a child learning to speak utters 'amba, amba' indistinctly, and by it those who know become certain about the distinct form —
Verse 1.151.commentary: The homeliest verse in the kanda, and the model for 1.152. The infant calls amba amba — 'mama' — avyaktam, indistinctly: the form is blurred, half-made. Yet tad-vidam, for those who know — the mother, the household — niscayah bhavati vyakte: certainty arises *about the distinct form*; the hearers pass through the blur to the word the child is reaching for, and understanding happens there, at the vyakta, not at the noise. The baby-talk case is perfect for its purpose because the mediation is undisguised: no one thinks the infant's syllables signify in their own right; everyone hears *through* them to amba — inference (1.149) with its workings still visible, before habit has hidden them. It also carries a quiet tenderness of doctrine: the child's fallen form is not censured but *completed* by the knowing hearer — which is what every hearer of apabhramsa does, and what the sista tradition does for the whole speech-community, holding the distinct forms on behalf of tongues still learning. Sanskrit itself, the verse implies, is the language's amba: what the blurred callings mean.
Verse 1.151.contested: none of substance; the verse is syntactically the clearest in the passage — its work is entirely in the analogy it prepares for 1.152

Verse 1.152.translation: So, where the correct form is to be employed, when a corrupt one is employed, some meaning is expressed by it with the correct form intervening.
Verse 1.152.commentary: The apodosis, and the semantics of corruption completed. Evam — as with the child: sadhau prayoktavye, where the sadhu form is the one *to be* employed (the normative participle carrying 1.139's ethics), the apabhramsa that actually issues expresses its meaning sadhu-vyavahitah — with the correct form *intervening*, standing between the corrupt sound and the meaning as the relay through which expression passes. Vyavahita is the doctrine's exact word: not 'replaced by' nor 'associated with' but *interposed* — the hearer's route runs gavi → gauh → cow, the middle term supplied by competence (the mother's knowledge in 1.151, the sista-smrti at large). This is the final settlement of the problem opened at 1.27: corrupt forms convey (conceded throughout), but their conveying is parasitic — borrowed light, the sadhu form doing the expressing from behind the screen. The theory has real explanatory reach: it predicts that apabhramsa comprehension varies with the hearer's command of the target forms (as it does), and it makes the norm *internal* to the semantics rather than an etiquette laid over it. 1.153 immediately tests the theory at its breaking point: communities where no target form remains.
Verse 1.152.contested: kascid arthah, 'some meaning', is lightly hedged — either 'a certain meaning' (the intended one, reached mediately) or a genuine qualification ('some meaning or other', imperfectly determinate); the first fits the child-analogy, the second anticipates 1.153's degraded-transmission case, and the hedge may be deliberately open

Verse 1.153.translation: When, through transmission among defective speakers, corrupt forms have attained establishment, for those speakers it is the correct form that is non-expressive.
Verse 1.153.commentary: The theory driven honestly into its hardest case — and yielding to it. Paramparyat: by transmission, generation upon generation, among vigunesu abhidhatrsu, speakers gone defective (communities severed from the sista line). There the apabhramsas prasiddhim agatah — have attained establishment, become the *current* forms, rudhi (1.128) now running entirely on the fallen side. And the verse does not flinch from the consequence: tesam, for those speakers, sadhur avacakah — it is the *correct* form that fails to express. The relay of 1.152 has inverted: where gavi is what everyone says and gauh is heard as strange noise, the mediation runs the other way or not at all; expressiveness follows establishment, and establishment follows transmission, wherever transmission goes. This is a remarkable concession for a normative grammarian — it grants the Prakrits their full semantic citizenship within their own communities and concedes that sadhutva confers no magical reach beyond the community that sustains it. What survives the concession is only, but exactly, the dharma-claim (1.27, 1.139): the established corrupt form expresses, in its own domain, as well as Sanskrit ever did — what it does not do is bear the unseen fruit. The norm has retreated, under pressure of honest linguistics, to the one ground (adrsta) where observation cannot follow to contest it.
Verse 1.153.contested: none syntactically; doctrinally, whether 1.153 describes a regrettable pathology or simply *how language communities work* is the live question — the verse's own even tone supports the descriptive reading, and 1.154 shows the school dividing over precisely this

Verse 1.154.translation: This divine speech has been overspread by incapable speakers; among the seers of non-eternality, in this matter, the judgement is the reverse.
Verse 1.154.commentary: The two ideologies of language change, set face to face. First the school's own telling: daivi vak — divine speech, the Sanskrit of the seed-tradition — vyatikirnā, overspread, scattered through, asaktaih abhidhatrbhih, by speakers without capacity (the vigunas of 1.153): change as corruption, history as the silting of a pure source. Then, with striking evenhandedness, the rival telling: anitya-darsinam — for the seers of non-eternality, those whose darsana makes impermanence fundamental (the Buddhists above all, though any anti-eternalist qualifies) — asmin vade buddhi-viparyayah: in this dispute their judgement is the exact reverse. On their view the current speech of peoples is the natural datum and 'divine Sanskrit' the artifact — a fixed, frozen elaboration raised out of living vernaculars; the direction of derivation flips with the metaphysics. Bhartrhari states the inversion without refuting it — consistent with 1.140's concession that about any agama the contrary can be alleged — and the honesty is structural: he has already granted (1.153) everything the rival needs descriptively; what separates the schools is not the linguistics but the eternality-metaphysics, and *that*, by his own epistemology, argument alone will not settle (1.34). The verse is perhaps the earliest clear statement that the Sanskrit-Prakrit priority dispute is theory-laden all the way down.
Verse 1.154.contested: vyatikirna we render 'overspread/scattered-through'; it admits 'mixed, adulterated' — the image of seed-crop overgrown; the identification of the anitya-darsins with Buddhists specifically is traditional and probable but the karika names no school

Verse 1.155.translation: Because both streams are unbroken, when a word is employed with the intention of another word, that word is not the expressor of the meaning.
Verse 1.155.commentary: The kanda's final karika in this recension of the text, resolving 1.154's standoff at the level of practice. Ubhayesam avicchedat — both lines, sadhu and apabhramsa, sista-transmission and vernacular transmission, are *unbroken*: each has the continuity that 1.31 and 1.141 made the touchstone of standing; neither can be dismissed as a mere lapse of the other (this much is 1.153-154 consolidated). The discrimination therefore falls back — as at 1.147 — on intention within employment: anya-sabda-vivaksaya yo 'nyah prayujyate sabdah — when one word is employed *with the intention of another word* (gavi uttered reaching for gauh, or indeed the reverse), na so 'rthasya abhidhayakah: that uttered word is not the expressor; the intended word is, through it (1.152). The doctrine's last word is thus neither the triumph of Sanskrit nor its relativization, but a criterion indifferent to which stream is 'first': expression belongs to the form the speaker's vivaksa actually deploys, and forms reached-through are conduits, not expressors. That this closes the Brahmakanda is fitting beyond its topic: the kanda began with the undivided word appearing as many (1.1) and ends with the everyday case of one word appearing through another — the whole metaphysics recapitulated in a grammarian's scruple about who, in the utterance, is really doing the meaning.
Verse 1.155.contested: the verse generalizes 1.147's intention-relativity, and its scope is debated: read narrowly it restates the apabhramsa doctrine; read broadly it covers *any* employment-through (citation, metalepsis, even the dhvani-sphota relation itself) — the placement as kanda-close invites the broad reading, and we incline to it; note also that the karika-count of Kanda I varies between recensions, wisdomlib's text ending at 155 while other editions carry additional closing verses
